P1-001.title: Lightcast: The Generative AI Job Market 2025 — Data & Insights
P1-001.author: Lightcast research team
P1-001.excerpt: Unique job postings requiring generative-AI skills grew from 55 (Jan 2021) to ~10,000 (May 2025). 2024 vs 2023 saw 66,000 GenAI-specific postings vs 16,000. Non-IT roles requiring AI skills are up 9x 2022-2024; other IT roles up 35x. 51% of AI postings in 2024 were outside IT/CS occupations (was 39% in 2019). 800% growth in generative-AI roles across non-tech industries since 2022. HR sector: 66% growth; Education/Training 200%; Finance 40%; Marketing/PR now 8% of postings AI-required, 50% annual growth. Based on 1.3B job postings.
P1-001.relevance: Anchor dataset for Trend 2 (AI skills as hiring criterion). Establishes the broad-based, cross-sector nature of the shift and the salary premium.
P1-001.credibility: high — Lightcast is the leading labor market analytics firm in this space; methodology is transparent and based on 1.3B postings.

P1-002.title: Lightcast: 28% salary premium / $18,000 for AI-skilled postings
P1-002.author: Lightcast
P1-002.excerpt: 28% higher salary for AI-skilled postings — approximately $18,000/year premium. Skills Disruption Matrix methodology: growth rate × importance × AI exposure across 350 AI skills in 9 clusters.
P1-002.relevance: Quantifies the market valuation of AI skills as a hiring asset — supports the JD-shift trend.
P1-002.credibility: high — same source as P1-001, peer-reviewed methodology.

P1-003.title: PwC 2025 Global AI Jobs Barometer — Press Release
P1-003.author: PwC global research
P1-003.excerpt: 56% wage premium for AI-skilled jobs, up from 25% the prior year. Jobs requiring AI skills grew 7.5% YoY even as total job postings fell 11.3%. Skills sought in AI-exposed occupations are changing 66% faster (was 25%). Productivity growth in AI-exposed industries: 7% (2018-2022) → 27% (2018-2024). Degree-required share for AI-augmented jobs fell from 66% to 59% (-7pp) from 2019 to 2024; for AI-automated jobs from 53% to 44% (-9pp). Analysed close to 1 billion job ads + thousands of company financial reports across six continents.
P1-003.relevance: The only major dataset that triangulates BOTH trends at once — AI-skill premium widening AND credential requirements softening in the same exposed-roles cohort.
P1-003.credibility: high — 1B postings, six continents, but discount the 56% premium partly for senior-skew composition effect.

P1-004.title: Growth Unhinged: Your next job will require AI skills
P1-004.author: Kyle Poyar
P1-004.excerpt: AI-skill-required postings in tech GTM roles grew from 65 (Jul 2023) to ~1,000 (Jul 2025) — a 1,438% increase in two years for non-engineering, customer-facing roles. Affected roles: social media producers, paid search specialists, BDRs, marketing analysts, content marketing managers, product marketers, CMOs. Example JD language: 'working proficiency of AI tools like ChatGPT' (bottom) to 'skills using AI copilots and agents to personalize messaging, automate outreach, and surface insights from CRM data' (top). Zapier: 89% of employees are now 'AI-fluent.' Companies hiring AI-native GTM: Clay, Vercel, Wiz, Zapier, Brex, ClickUp.
P1-004.relevance: Strongest evidence that AI literacy is a hard JD requirement OUTSIDE engineering, with role-level granularity that Lightcast's macro data lacks.
P1-004.credibility: high — Kyle Poyar is a credible GTM analyst (ex-OpenView Partners); data is self-collected from job boards.

P1-005.title: Augment Code: How we hire AI-native engineers now
P1-005.author: Augment Code engineering leadership
P1-005.excerpt: Coding ability is no longer the primary differentiator of engineering talent. Six dimensions now evaluated: product/outcome taste, system/architectural judgment, agent leverage (structuring work so AI agents execute well), communication, ownership, learning velocity. 'The highest-leverage engineer isn't the one who writes the most code. It's the one who ensures the team is solving the right problem.' 'Think of it as delegation — except your reports are incredibly fast and occasionally confidently wrong.' Four hiring profiles: AI-Native Systems Engineer, Product Engineer, Applied AI Engineer, Early Professional.
P1-005.relevance: Concrete employer voice — they have moved past code-as-portfolio toward 'judgment + agent leverage' evaluation. The evidence they want is hard to convey via static profile.
P1-005.credibility: medium-high — first-person employer statement, but Augment has commercial interest in 'AI-native engineering' framing.

P1-006.title: Axios: AI threatens Big Law's talent pipeline
P1-006.author: Axios staff
P1-006.excerpt: 2025 legal market reports show law firms reduced the pace of associate hiring or trimmed summer associate programs because AI handles the doc-review/research/contract-drafting grunt work that used to train juniors. Firms now want experienced laterals who can step into live matters from day one. Career professionals at law schools say 'AI proficiency' makes candidates more attractive. David Freeman Engstrom (Stanford Law): 'Getting ready for a world in which you need fewer human lawyers.' Nik Guggenberger (U. Houston Law): 'If more and more of that work that trains junior associates is being automated, then there's no real material anymore for them to train on.' Lawyer role reframed as 'symphony conductor' who orchestrates AI outputs and legal analysis.
P1-006.relevance: Proof that AI-skill premium is hitting deeply credential-gated industries. Critical nuance: AI competence is LAYERED ON TOP OF, not in place of, credentials in regulated industries.
P1-006.credibility: high — Axios news article with named academic sources.

P1-007.title: Fortune: AI could eliminate entry-level Wall Street banking jobs
P1-007.author: Sydney Lake
P1-007.excerpt: Big firms considering pulling back junior banker hiring by up to two-thirds as Wall Street relies more on AI. Christoph Rabenseifner (Deutsche Bank Chief Strategy Officer for Tech/Data/Innovation): 'The easy idea is you just replace juniors with an AI tool.' Jeanne Branthover (DHR Global): 'It's like being in a fraternity and being a pledge. You gotta put in the work to get upstairs.' Michael Ashley Schulman (Running Point): 'AI won't eliminate entry-level Wall Street jobs, but it will reduce the number of heads required.' Counter-voice Tim Bates (U. Michigan): AI is 'reshaping' roles rather than replacing them.
P1-007.relevance: Same pipeline-thinning pattern as Big Law — confirms it's not industry-specific but a feature of all credential-gated apprenticeship industries.
P1-007.credibility: high — Fortune reporting with multiple named senior executives.

P1-008.title: Fortune: Wall Street layoffs may be more hype than takeover
P1-008.author: Emma Burleigh
P1-008.excerpt: Robert Seamans (NYU Stern Center for the Future of Management): 'AI is often a scapegoat for things, because it's easier to blame AI than softening consumer demand.' Pim Hilbers (BCG): 'I think the general headcount trend in banking over the last decade is stable to slightly declining.' Daniel Keum (Columbia): 'Banking consulting actually not doing too bad' due to low automation tolerance. Hard data: 92% of Columbia MBA class 2025 received job offers; Harvard MBA offer-less graduates rose from 4% (2021) to 15% (2024); Goldman Sachs headcount grew (48,300 Sept 2025 vs 46,500 Sept 2024); JPMorgan added 2,000 employees.
P1-008.relevance: Critical contrarian voice — the AI-replaces-juniors story is partly narrative, with headcount data telling a different story. Necessary for balanced reporting.
P1-008.credibility: high — multiple named academic and consulting experts, hard headcount numbers.

P1-009.title: Indeed Hiring Lab: AI at Work Report 2025
P1-009.author: Indeed Hiring Lab
P1-009.excerpt: Job-level distribution: 26% of US jobs face 'high' GenAI transformation potential, 54% moderate, 20% low. Skill-level: 46% hybrid (human+AI), 40% minimal, 19% assisted, 1% fully transformable (~19 skills 'very likely' to be fully replaced — up from 0 a year prior). Software dev: 81% of skills are hybrid-transformation. Nursing: 68% minimal but 14% of postings — admin tasks — are hybrid-transformation. Methodology: ~2,900 skills evaluated by GPT-4.1 + Claude Sonnet 4 dual evaluation. Key thesis: 'Work skills themselves won't change, but how they are applied almost certainly will. Credentials may need to evolve rather than be replaced.'
P1-009.relevance: Most balanced framing — argues against clean 'credentials → portfolios' replacement story. Useful for limitations section as a corrective to maximalist claims.
P1-009.credibility: high — Indeed Hiring Lab, transparent methodology.

P1-010.title: Greenhouse 2025 Workforce & Hiring Report
P1-010.author: Greenhouse Software
P1-010.excerpt: 28% of candidates admit using AI to generate fake work samples. 32% claimed AI skills they don't actually have. 45% use AI to prepare for interviews. 22% use bots to auto-apply (31% for Gen Z). 36% of US job seekers altered appearance/voice/background in video interviews. 91% US hiring managers have encountered or suspected AI-generated interview answers. 74% more concerned about fake credentials/deepfakes than a year ago. 72% report job differed materially from posting. 46% of US job seekers say trust in hiring has decreased; 42% blame AI. CEO Daniel Chait: 'Intent will be the new differentiator in hiring.' 'We don't need more hoops or friction; we need better signals.'
P1-010.relevance: Strongest empirical signal-collapse evidence. AI-polished resumes are losing signal value — but AI-generated work samples are now ALSO part of the fraud surface. New trust scaffolding is needed.
P1-010.credibility: high — Greenhouse runs hiring software at scale, methodology is candidate + manager surveys.

P1-011.title: Fortune: Trust at all-time low — talent acquisition is in an 'AI doom loop'
P1-011.author: Nino Paoli
P1-011.excerpt: Daniel Chait, Greenhouse CEO: 'This is the first time I can remember where both sides were unhappy.' 'Trust is at an all-time low for both job seekers and recruiters.' 'You end up basically not being able to tell anyone apart.' Paddy Lambros, Dex CEO: 'I can understand the desire for candidates to use AI tools. I just don't think it's very effective.' 'AI usage in first-round interviews is downright insulting and inhumane.' Hard numbers: LinkedIn applications +45% YoY hitting 11,000/minute (June 2025); 41% of US job seekers admit using prompt injections to bypass AI filters; 75% use AI to polish applications; only 8% of job seekers believe AI screening is fair vs 70% of hiring managers who trust it.
P1-011.relevance: The keystone signal-collapse citation. Resume-as-signal is dying not because portfolios won but because AI saturated the channel. New identity layers must be trust-bearing from day one.
P1-011.credibility: high — Fortune reporting, multiple named CEOs.

P1-012.title: Greenhouse newsroom: AI Trust Crisis statistics
P1-012.author: Greenhouse
P1-012.excerpt: 70% of hiring managers trust AI for faster, better hiring decisions; only 8% of job seekers think AI screening is fair. 46% of job seekers report decreased trust in hiring; 42% blame AI directly. 62% of Gen-Z entry-level workers lost trust. 41% admit using prompt injections; 52% of non-users considering this tactic. 65% of hiring managers caught applicants using AI deceptively (32% reading from AI scripts, 22% hiding prompt injections in resumes, 18% appearing as deepfakes). 36% altered appearance/voice/background in video interviews (59% to appear more professional, 37% to hide identity). Daniel Chait: 'It's an AI doom loop that's getting worse, not better.'
P1-012.relevance: The single largest stat dump on the asymmetric AI trust gap and candidate gaming behavior. Critical for both 'paradigm shift' framing and 'limitations' calibration.
P1-012.credibility: high — primary source (Greenhouse own data), large sample.

P1-013.title: Burning Glass Institute × HBS: Skills-Based Hiring — From Pronouncements to Practice
P1-013.author: Joseph Fuller (HBS) + Burning Glass Institute
P1-013.excerpt: Analysed online job postings 2014-2023, 11,300 roles at large firms. ~400% increase in annual roles where employers removed degree requirements over the decade. BUT: hiring of workers without a BA increased only 3.5 percentage points on average in affected roles. Net economy effect: ~97,000 additional non-degree hires out of 77M yearly hires in 2023 — fewer than 1 in 700. Net 0.14pp increase in non-degree hiring share. Three employer categories: Leaders (37%, ~20% more non-degree hires), In-Name-Only (45%, announcements but no actual change), Backsliders (18%, initial drop then reversal). At leaders: 10pp higher retention, 25% salary increase for non-degree workers. Joseph Fuller: 'We found measurable but pretty modest changes... Changing your hiring policy is, at best, the end of the beginning.'
P1-013.relevance: Strongest cold-evidence pushback against the 'skills/works > credentials' narrative. The PR announcements are loud, the actual hiring change is small. Critical contrarian datapoint.
P1-013.credibility: high — Harvard Business School + Burning Glass, methodology peer-reviewed.

P1-014.title: Hacker News: Ask HN — With AI-written resumes, what are the new hiring signals?
P1-014.author: Various HN commenters; lead voice: scarface_74
P1-014.excerpt: scarface_74 (top-voted): 'If you are blindly submitting your resume to an ATS, you've already lost.' Reports direct outreach via recruiters + LinkedIn yielded multiple offers in weeks; hundreds of cold applications yielded nothing. Same commenter notes that being 'the second highest contributor to a popular official AWS solution' opened doors while other OSS work went unnoticed — 'signal density matters.' Other themes: excessive resume polish itself signals AI generation ('correct but not too polished'); referrals aren't a silver bullet; 'bounty-to-hire' emerging as alt channel.
P1-014.relevance: Voice-of-engineer calibration. HN already lives post-resume but the REPLACEMENT is mostly network/referral, not portfolio. Portfolio is necessary-not-sufficient.
P1-014.credibility: medium — HN community is high-agency, high-network, NOT representative of broader workforce. Useful as signal of where the curve is heading, not where the mean is.

P1-015.title: Figma blog: Why demand for designers is on the rise
P1-015.author: Figma
P1-015.excerpt: 73% of hiring managers see an increasing need for candidates proficient in AI tools; 79% say designing AI products is essential. 58% cite visual polish as top-5 skill; 45%+ cite collaboration, systems thinking, product strategy. 56% of hiring managers report increased demand for SENIOR designers vs only 25% for junior roles. UK-based tech hiring manager quoted: 'We fully prioritize positions that combine technical capacity, strategy, and out-of-the-box thinking.' Daniel Wert (search firm CEO) warns against 'unicorn' hiring: 'multiple people with complementary strengths — not a single superhero.'
P1-015.relevance: Confirms AI fluency is a hard requirement in creative roles (not just tech). Also evidence of junior-design pipeline thinning, same pattern as law/finance.
P1-015.credibility: medium-high — Figma has product interest in 'designers + AI' framing but the survey numbers are specific and consistent with other sources.

P1-016.title: hubbedin.com: The UX/UI Hiring Shift — What Employers Want in 2025
P1-016.author: hubbedin recruiting team
P1-016.excerpt: Portfolio (where the signal lives in 2025): case studies with problem→process→impact structure, collaborative evidence (annotated artifacts, FigJam boards), metrics showing design results, interactive prototypes, video walkthroughs, personal design philosophy. Resume (supporting role): cross-functional collaboration, business metrics literacy, tool proficiency, soft-skill stories. Key prescription: 'Employers want designers who can demonstrate WHY a design decision was made, not just how it looks.' 'It's not about listing tools — it's about demonstrating how you use them to collaborate, test, and iterate.'
P1-016.relevance: Closest mainstream 2025 source asserting portfolio-leads-resume hierarchy for designers. Concrete prescription of what the portfolio must contain.
P1-016.credibility: medium — recruiting blog, no primary research. Directionally consistent with Figma data.

P1-017.title: Metaview: Beyond the hype — How AI is actually transforming recruiting in 2025
P1-017.author: Metaview team
P1-017.excerpt: 40% of talent specialists worry that automation will make candidate experience feel cold and impersonal. 35% fear AI will overlook candidates with unique skills and experiences. LinkedIn 2025 research found companies using AI-assisted messaging are 9% more likely to make a quality hire — modest improvement vs hype.
P1-017.relevance: Recruiter-side skepticism data point. Counters the 'AI is solving hiring' narrative — gains are real but small.
P1-017.credibility: medium — vendor blog with commercial interest in 'humanized AI hiring,' but data points are sourced.

P1-018.title: MIT Technology Review: The great AI hype correction of 2025
P1-018.author: Will Douglas Heaven
P1-018.excerpt: 2025 represents a necessary correction period where inflated AI expectations are being reset. MIT researchers found 95% of businesses trying AI implementations discovered ZERO value (study measured bespoke systems past 6-month pilot; excluded informal employee use). Andrej Karpathy: chatbots 'outperform average humans in many areas but are not better than an expert human' — explains limited workplace disruption.
P1-018.relevance: Macro-level skeptic voice. Important calibration: AI in JD language is growing fast, but operational AI value in companies is contested.
P1-018.credibility: high — MIT Tech Review with specific cited research.

P1-019.title: GCheck Report: 63% of workers admit exaggerating AI skills
P1-019.author: GCheck (background-check vendor)
P1-019.excerpt: 63% of workers admit to exaggerating AI skills on resumes/profiles — driven by automation anxiety. Up from Greenhouse's mid-2025 figure of 32% claiming false AI proficiency, suggesting the gap is widening as AI requirements proliferate in JDs.
P1-019.relevance: Confirms candidate-side AI-skill inflation is accelerating. Important for credibility-of-self-report calibration: 'AI skills' on resumes are unreliable signals.
P1-019.credibility: medium — vendor-published with commercial interest in selling background checks, but the directional finding aligns with Greenhouse independent data.

P1-020.title: Axios: The dimming job market's bright spot — AI skills
P1-020.author: Axios staff
P1-020.excerpt: AI skills are the singular bright spot in an otherwise softening US labor market. Jobs requiring AI skills are growing while overall postings are declining. Wage premium for AI-skilled jobs is widening, not narrowing.
P1-020.relevance: Macro convergence point — multiple datasets (PwC, Lightcast, Indeed, Axios) agree AI-skill posting growth is real and durable through late 2025.

P1-021.title: Stripe job listing: AEO/GEO Marketing Manager
P1-021.author: Stripe
P1-021.excerpt: Stripe explicitly hiring an 'AEO/GEO Marketing Manager' (Answer Engine Optimization / Generative Engine Optimization) — focused on driving growth from ChatGPT and Perplexity. Role didn't exist 18 months ago.
P1-021.relevance: Concrete JD evidence — top-tier company has invented a new role category native to LLM-driven discovery. Strong proof of 'new hiring categories' emerging.
P1-021.credibility: high — primary source, live job posting.

P1-022.title: Kevan Lee: AI Marketing Manager — job description
P1-022.author: Kevan Lee (ex-Buffer marketing leader)
P1-022.excerpt: Provides a template JD for an 'AI Marketing Manager' — a new role category. Typical responsibilities: act as subject matter expert in applying generative AI to marketing, reduce repetitive work, increase campaign velocity. Skills section requires demonstrable use of ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Midjourney; expects portfolio of AI-augmented marketing artifacts.
P1-022.relevance: Concrete demonstration that 'AI-using marketer' is now a JD category, not just a bonus skill. Backs Growth Unhinged's broader thesis at the individual-JD level.
P1-022.credibility: medium — credible industry voice, but template JD not deployed hiring data.

P1-023.title: Nursing Outlook (2025): N.U.R.S.E.S. framework for AI literacy in nursing
P1-023.author: Nursing Outlook editorial team
P1-023.excerpt: Landmark 2025 framework for AI literacy in nursing: Navigate AI basics, Utilize AI strategically, Recognize AI pitfalls, Skills support, Ethics in action, Shape the future. American Association of Colleges of Nursing updated its 2025 Essentials framework to explicitly include informatics + AI competencies for new RNs. Companion 2025 Frontiers in Medicine meta-analysis: 'institutional capacity to deliver structured AI training remains severely underdeveloped' despite high individual interest.
P1-023.relevance: Even in deeply credential-gated nursing profession (RN license required), AI competency is now stated baseline competency. AI skills layer ON TOP of credentials — they don't replace them.
P1-023.credibility: high — peer-reviewed nursing literature + accreditation body framework.

P1-024.title: Indeed standardized JD template: AI Content Writer
P1-024.author: Indeed
P1-024.excerpt: Standardized JD template requires: 'Knowledge of natural language processing, AI, and machine learning technologies'; 'Stay current on SEO, content marketing, AI search trends'; 'Use generative AI tools (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Midjourney) to scale content production.' 90% of content marketers already use AI in workflow (2025 data cited in template).
P1-024.relevance: Indeed's standardized template = de facto industry consensus on what an AI content writer JD looks like. AI tool proficiency is no longer a differentiator — it's the floor.
P1-024.credibility: high — Indeed shapes how millions of JDs get written.

P1-025.title: OpenAI live JD: Product Marketing Manager, ChatGPT for Work
P1-025.author: OpenAI
P1-025.excerpt: Live JD: 'Translate complex technical AI concepts to user-centric messaging.' Strong understanding of AI technologies required; ability to articulate AI value to technical AND non-technical audiences.
P1-025.relevance: Concrete primary-source JD for an AI-native marketing role at the most prominent AI company. Shows the exact language pattern: AI fluency required for non-engineering roles at scale.

P1-026.title: Indeed: Prompt Engineer JD template (2025/2026)
P1-026.author: Indeed
P1-026.excerpt: Standardized template: no required computer-related degree. 'What matters most: demonstrable skill with AI tools and a portfolio showcasing prompt engineering abilities.' Backgrounds welcomed: computer science, data science, linguistics, journalism, creative writing.
P1-026.relevance: Indeed itself declaring 'portfolio' as the primary signal for a high-growth new role category. This is one of the strongest cases of portfolio-over-credential in mainstream JD language.

P1-027.title: HR Dive: Employers failing at skills-based hiring
P1-027.author: HR Dive reporting on Burning Glass/HBS study
P1-027.excerpt: Report text: 'Our analysis makes clear that successful adoption of skills-based hiring involves more than simply stripping language from job postings'; needs 'robust and intentional changes.' Joseph Fuller: 'We found measurable but pretty modest changes, and that some companies have done better than others.'
P1-027.relevance: Industry-press confirmation of the Burning Glass headline — skills-based hiring is mostly performative.

P1-028.title: CBS News reporting on Brookings/Lightcast AI postings analysis
P1-028.author: CBS News
P1-028.excerpt: 100% YoY increase in AI-themed postings as of mid-2025. 29% avg annual growth over 15 years vs 11% for general economy. 80,000+ postings mentioned generative AI in 2025 vs 3,780 in 2010. Manufacturing AI adoption: 4% (early 2023) → 9% (mid-2025). Silicon Valley: 13% of all AI postings; Seattle 7%. Goldman Sachs Joseph Briggs: 'AI is definitely visible in the micro labor market data, but it doesn't look like it's driving the overall labor market dynamic.'
P1-028.relevance: Mainstream-press triangulation of Lightcast data + Goldman quote as macro calibration: AI is loud in JD language but quiet in aggregate labor flows.
P1-028.credibility: high — major news outlet, sourced to Brookings + Lightcast.

P1-029.title: UNLEASH: Greenhouse — 28% of candidates use AI to generate fake work samples
P1-029.author: UNLEASH reporting on Greenhouse data
P1-029.excerpt: 28% of candidates admit using AI to generate fake work samples. 47% embellish qualifications. AI-generated 'portfolios' are themselves part of the fraud surface, not just AI-generated cover letters.
P1-029.relevance: Critical caveat for the 'portfolio replaces resume' thesis: portfolios are ALSO AI-fakable. Any new product must solve trust verification, not just rich-media display.
P1-029.credibility: high — secondary reporting on Greenhouse primary data.

P1-030.title: HR Tech Edge: Greenhouse report — hiring system in crisis
P1-030.author: HR Tech Edge
P1-030.excerpt: 80% of US workers feel insecure in current roles. 72% report jobs differed materially from posting. 26% (45% Gen Z) say AI has made it harder to stand out. Paaras Parker (Greenhouse CPO): 'Transparency, communication, and fairness are competitive advantages.'
P1-030.relevance: Crisis-of-trust framing — the OPPORTUNITY for a portfolio/identity product is real, but only if it ships trust, not just features.
P1-030.credibility: medium — industry blog, but sourced to Greenhouse primary data.

P1-031.title: Lightcast: Beyond the Buzz press release
P1-031.author: Lightcast / Elena Magrini
P1-031.excerpt: Elena Magrini (Lightcast): emphasises the range from 'advanced AI-specific roles, such as AI engineers, to more general AI-related positions such as software developers.' Total 800% growth in generative-AI roles across non-tech industries since 2022. HR sector growth: 66% (leading all sectors).
P1-031.relevance: Authoritative voice on the breadth (not just depth) of AI-skill demand. Important: it's NOT all AI-engineer roles; it's mostly AI-using-while-doing-something-else roles.

P1-032.title: Recruiter-perspective synthesis: LinkedIn or Resume in 2025
P1-032.author: edzer career consulting
P1-032.excerpt: 'Recruiters almost never read your resume without LinkedIn open on the second monitor.' 87% of recruiters use LinkedIn to find candidates, often ignoring the application stack altogether. 'Recruiters spend less time reading resumes. They're Googling you. They're checking if you've shipped something, contributed to something, or written something worth reading.' Uses 'triangulation' — comparing resume with LinkedIn and other public data to verify authenticity.
P1-032.relevance: The practical mechanic of how hiring decisions are actually being made in 2025: triangulation across multiple surfaces. This points to an identity-aggregation opportunity, NOT a LinkedIn-replacement opportunity.
P1-032.credibility: medium — career consulting blog, but consistent with multiple other sources.

P1-033.title: HBS: Joseph Fuller on the college degree gap
P1-033.author: Joseph Fuller / HBS Working Knowledge
P1-033.excerpt: Among 'Skills-Based Hiring Leader' companies: non-degree hires showed 10pp higher retention and 25% average salary increase entering roles previously requiring a BA. Named leaders: Apple, Cigna, ExxonMobil, GM, Koch Industries, Target, Tyson Foods, Walmart, plus government employers (Minnesota, Denver).
P1-033.relevance: When companies actually DO skills-based hiring (not just announce it), the candidate-side outcomes are excellent. This is the existence proof that the framework works — just rarely implemented.

P1-034.title: HN Trends: AI mentions in 'Who is hiring' posts
P1-034.author: HN Trends
P1-034.excerpt: May 2026 saw a 35% improvement in hiring posts over April, marking the first 400+ posting month since May 2023. AI is mentioned in nearly 25% of job postings now and is the top technology term. Applicants are often expected to provide portfolios and GitHub profiles; some positions require 'a public GitHub or portfolio with shipped projects, and the ability to ship a complex tool in under a day.'
P1-034.relevance: Hard data from a programmer-skewed but high-quality corpus: portfolio/GitHub is now table stakes in HN hiring posts; AI mentions are at 25% saturation.
P1-034.credibility: high — HN Trends scrapes primary data.

P1-035.title: HN Hiring (May 2026) — Sample of AI-required JD language
P1-035.author: HN Hiring aggregator
P1-035.excerpt: Multiple May 2026 'Who is hiring' postings explicitly require: 'public GitHub or portfolio with shipped projects, ability to ship a complex tool in under a day.' Applicants asked to 'reply in-thread with links to your GitHub/LinkedIn/portfolio.'
P1-035.relevance: Primary JD-language evidence from the hiring corpus most aligned with our hypothesized user behavior.
P1-035.credibility: medium-high — primary corpus but skewed to small/AI-startups.

P1-036.title: HR Dive: AI race has fostered both better hiring decisions and mistrust
P1-036.author: HR Dive
P1-036.excerpt: AI in recruiting is widely adopted, but real-world results are proving more modest than initial promises. 65% of large firms mention AI-related skills in JDs vs 45% of mid-sized firms — implying firm-size composition effect in aggregate AI-skill posting growth.
P1-036.relevance: Important calibration: the AI-skill-required JD trend is concentrated at large firms. Aggregate numbers overstate the experience of a typical small-firm jobseeker.

P1-037.title: Medium (Shubham Sharma): Why hiring managers should look at GitHub profiles
P1-037.author: Shubham Sharma
P1-037.excerpt: Argues for thoughtful, nuanced evaluation of candidate GitHub profile combined with other assessment methods. 'GitHub contributions should not be the sole determinant in hiring decisions, especially for junior developers.' 'Hiring managers should utilise this information more intelligently, ethically, and as one piece of a larger puzzle rather than relying on GitHub activity alone.'
P1-037.relevance: Practitioner pushback against GitHub-as-portfolio-proxy. Important nuance: even where portfolio signals exist, they're inputs not verdicts.
P1-037.credibility: low-medium — individual practitioner blog, but echoes the broader consensus around triangulation.

P1-038.title: PwC 2025 Global AI Jobs Barometer — full report
P1-038.author: PwC global research
P1-038.excerpt: Skills sought in AI-exposed occupations are changing 66% faster than in non-exposed roles (was 25% the year prior). The half-life of skills is collapsing where AI is present. 3x higher growth in revenue per employee in most-exposed industries.
P1-038.relevance: Critical for the 'capability is the new evidence' framing — credentials describing what you knew last year are decaying faster than ever.

P1-039.title: Entrepreneur: AI is leading to a 'doom loop' for job seekers
P1-039.author: Entrepreneur staff
P1-039.excerpt: Companies are suddenly inundated with four to five times more job applications than they had just a month prior. The core problem: employers struggle with being overwhelmed by tons of applicants and can't tell which ones to pay attention to, while job seekers find it easier than ever to apply but harder to actually get hired.
P1-039.relevance: Quantifies the signal-collapse: 4-5x application volume increase in a single month. Resume-as-screening is now mathematically broken.
P1-039.credibility: medium — Entrepreneur news, sourced to Greenhouse/LinkedIn data.

P1-040.title: HR Dive: Hiring is in 'an AI doom loop,' Greenhouse CEO says
P1-040.author: HR Dive
P1-040.excerpt: Daniel Chait reiterates: 'Intent will be the new differentiator in hiring.' The implication: anything that genuinely conveys candidate intent and effort — a portfolio with verifiable provenance, a thoughtfully-curated profile of real work — has elevated value. But static resumes and AI-polished applications no longer do this.
P1-040.relevance: Best phrasing of the underlying market problem we're trying to solve: hiring needs better signals of real intent and effort.

P1-041.title: PR Newswire (Greenhouse): Only 7% of candidates say job market favors them
P1-041.author: Greenhouse via PR Newswire
P1-041.excerpt: Only 7% of candidates say the job market favors them. Major challenges: ageism, AI cheating, ghosting (jobs disappearing after applications). 22% of candidates report using AI auto-apply bots (31% Gen Z).
P1-041.relevance: Candidate-side desperation context. The 22-31% auto-apply rate is what's driving the volume explosion that recruiters complain about. Feedback loop on both sides.

P1-042.title: NursesEducator: 8 essential AI competencies for nurses by 2026
P1-042.author: NursesEducator editorial
P1-042.excerpt: September 2025 study found 71% of US hospitals had implemented predictive AI models. 'AI literacy is no longer an optional specialization — it is a foundational expert competency... not optional — it is the access requirement for secure and able exercise in digitally included healthcare environments.'
P1-042.relevance: Confirms healthcare AI requirements are real (71% hospital adoption). The competency expectation now extends to bedside RNs, not just specialists.
P1-042.credibility: medium — vocational education blog, but sourced to AHA hospital adoption data.
